news.title: Who were the 4 US soldiers killed in Iranian attacks over the weekend?
news.text:
Following the collapse of the fragile ceasefire deal between the United States and Iran, scores of American troops have been injured and at least four service members have been killed in escalated attacks in the Middle East.

The recent deaths and injuries over the weekend bring the toll to at least 18 U.S. service members who have been killed since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes against Iran on Feb. 28, and roughly 500 have been wounded, according to Defense Department figures, including an updated casualty count provided Monday by Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell. 

Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York, is believed to have been killed in action on July 17, 2026, during an enemy attack at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base, Jordan, the Pentagon announced on Tuesday night.

U.S. Central Command previously announced this Soldier as missing.

Sgt. Rampersad was assigned to 1st Battalion, 57th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 52nd Air Defense Artillery Brigade, 10th Army Air & Missile Defense Command, Ansbach, Germany.

The rising casualties show Iran maintains the ability to strike U.S. bases and inflict significant damage, despite repeated assertions by President Donald Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth that Tehran's military had been effectively destroyed. Hegseth declared the campaign a "historic and overwhelming victory" during an April press conference.

Here's a look at the other three soldiers who the Pentagon has identified as being killed after the collapse of the ceasefire and return to daily fighting. 

Pfc. Isabella Gonzales

Pfc. Isabella Gonzales, 19, enlisted in the Army last year and served as an air and missile defense crewmember maintaining and using the systems which have been key to protecting U.S. bases from Iranian attacks, according to the Army. 

She was killed Friday during an Iranian missile and drone attack at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan. She was assigned to 1st Battalion, 57th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 10th Army Air and Missile Defense Command.

"We are profoundly saddened by the loss of Pvt. Isabella Gonzales," Brig. Gen. Glenn Henke, commander of the 10th Army Air and Missile Defense Command, said in a statement. "She gave the ultimate sacrifice for her country and will always be remembered as a hero. Our hearts go out to her family, friends, and teammates. Our immediate priority is ensuring they have the resources and support they need to process this grief together."

Gonzales was posthumously promoted to private first class.

1st Lt. Tyler Feehan

1st Lt. Tyler Feehan, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, died from wounds suffered during that Friday attack on the Jordan air base the following day. 

He enlisted in the Army in 2019 as a combat engineer with the Hawaii Army National Guard, according to the service. He later commissioned from Brigham Young University as an active-duty Air Defense Artillery Officer in 2024. He was assigned to 2nd Battalion, 55th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 32nd Army Air and Missile Defense Command at Fort Bragg, North Carolina.

"1st Lt. Feehan's unwavering dedication to our mission, our values, and the air defenders he led will leave an enduring legacy," Brig. Gen. John Dawber, who leads the 32nd Air and Missile Defense Command, said in a statement. "We stand fully committed to supporting his family and loved ones through this incredibly difficult time, and we will never forget his ultimate sacrifice in defense of our nation."

Feehan will be posthumously promoted to captain, according to the service.

Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton

Army Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of Fayetteville, North Carolina, died Sunday following the controlled detonation of a downed Iranian attack drone at Erbil Air Base in Iraq, the Army said.

"Michael was an amazing man," his wife, Mia Gonzalez-Swinton, told ABC News in a statement. "He was an amazing father and an amazing husband. He cared so much for his soldiers, too."

Swinton was assigned to D Battery, 2nd Battalion, 55th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 108th Air Defense Artillery Brigade, at Fort Bragg, North Carolina. He served as an early warning systems operator, responsible for detecting, tracking and reporting incoming enemy threats to help protect U.S. forces from missile and drone attacks.

It remains unclear what led to Swinton's death. The use of controlled detonations is a common practice among trained explosive ordnance disposal teams to neutralize hazardous devices. Those procedures were refined over two decades of combat in Iraq and Afghanistan, where roadside bombs were a persistent threat to coalition forces. While the disposal of munitions is inherently dangerous, one U.S. official explained, fatal incidents are relatively rare. 

The Defense Department said an investigation is ongoing.

The Army said Swinton will be posthumously promoted to staff sergeant.

Base infrastructure

While U.S. forces are equipped with several high-tech systems to down Iranian missiles and drones, those attacks, once those weapons break through, troops often have limited protection from the infrastructure where they live and work.

For much of the post-9/11 era, U.S. bases in the Middle East outside of Iraq and Afghanistan were broadly viewed as safe, being divorced from those conflicts and firmly in support roles. Overseas installations rely on above-ground structures built from low-cost materials, including trailers and shipping containers, sometimes surrounded by concrete barriers or reinforced with sandbags on roofs or along exterior walls, protections which are not built to withstand aerial attacks or heavy explosives.

Even the large concrete blast walls that became a defining feature of U.S. bases during the Iraq and Afghanistan wars offer limited protection against direct strikes from modern missiles and drones. 

Those barriers were primarily designed to protect against gunfire, mortar and rocket attacks, and vehicle bombs detonated at a distance, not direct impacts from advanced weapons, according to a 2006 Army assessment of base infrastructure.

In recent years, defense officials have increasingly studied how to harden bases against drone attacks, recognizing that infrastructure built for earlier conflicts is often ill-suited to withstand modern threats.
news.author: Steven Beynon

news.title: He was fired by the White House right after being sworn in. Now a top federal prosecutor is suing
news.text:
The top federal prosecutor in Seattle who was fired last week less than an hour after being sworn in sued over his termination Tuesday, teeing up a legal challenge to the Trump administration's efforts to control who occupies the powerful Justice Department posts.

Roger Rogoff is the latest in a series of judicially appointed U.S. attorneys to be ousted by the Trump administration from their posts but is believed to be the first of that group to sue over the removal. His action creates a first-of-its-kind court case that will test the Justice Department's authority to fire federal prosecutors who are appointed by judges,

Rogoff was unanimously appointed to the job of U.S. attorney for western Washington by the federal judges in the district. But shortly after he was sworn in last Wednesday, he received an email from the Trump administration informing him that President Donald Trump had ordered his termination.

The lawsuit contends that the abrupt firing was unconstitutional and disregarded the authority of the judges of the district to name Rogoff to the job until the vacancy could be filled through a Senate-confirmed nominee. The complaint seeks a court order declaring that the firing “was unlawful and is void” and that Rogoff at least temporarily remains U.S. attorney.

“The President’s actions violate the law, and they ignore the protections of the Constitution of the United States," Rogoff said in a statement issued through the law firm representing him. "His removal of me, like his removal of other court-appointed U.S. attorneys across the country, is illegal and cannot stand."

 US attorneys are typically nominated by the president 

U.S. attorneys, top federal prosecutors who oversee regional Justice Department outposts across the country, are typically nominated by the president and then confirmed by the Senate. 

In the case of a vacancy, attorneys general have the authority to name an interim U.S. attorney who can serve for 120 days, but when those temporary appointments expire, the judges in a judicial district are then empowered to name a U.S. attorney — as happened in Rogoff's case — who can serve until the selection of a Senate-confirmed appointee.

But under Trump, the Justice Department has sought to leave unconfirmed prosecutors in their positions indefinitely or has moved to fire prosecutors appointed by the courts.

 The acting attorney general had foreshadowed the firing 

Rogoff's district has not had a Senate-confirmed U.S. attorney since 2023. He submitted his name for consideration with the federal bench after the judges last January signaled their intent to fill a vacancy created by the end of the 120-day term of Charles Neil Floyd, who had been named to the job in an interim capacity last year by then-Attorney General Pam Bondi.

Even before Rogoff was appointed, the lawsuit notes, acting Attorney General Todd Blanche foreshadowed his plans to fire whomever the judges selected, writing on X after the court announced its appointment of a merit selection panel that the candidates did not have the president’s support, “and I expect they will suffer the same fate as others have when judges ignore Article II” of the Constitution.

After Rogoff was fired, Blanche posted: “District court judges can appoint a temporary U.S. Attorney, and POTUS can fire them.” Judges from the state’s western district, he said, “abandoned the time-honored process of consultation with the administration so that the selected U.S. Attorney is qualified to serve in the administration. Roger Rogoff has been fired by the President.”

The Justice Department said in a statement Tuesday that “the district court did not coordinate with DOJ on this selection. Consistent with other firings of this type, this is wholly within the President’s authority.”

Other jurisdictions that have seen tussles between the judiciary and the administration over the prosecution posts include New Jersey, where Trump's former personal lawyer, Alina Habba, resigned in December after an appeals court said she had been serving in the post unlawfully.

Lindsey Halligan, another of Trump's personal lawyers who was installed by the administration, left her position as an acting U.S. attorney in Virginia after a judge concluded her appointment was unlawful and that indictments she brought against New York Attorney General Letitia James and former FBI Director James Comey must be dismissed.
news.summary: The top federal prosecutor in Seattle who was fired last week less than an hour after being sworn in is suing the government over his termination
news.author: Eric Tucker

news.title: US sends first tranche of $100 million aid package to Cuba
news.text:
The United States on Tuesday dispatched the first shipment of a proposed $100 million humanitarian aid package to Cuba, as the island continues to grapple with a deepening economic crisis.
According to the US State Department, a flight carrying pre-packaged food and hygiene kits for up to 700 Cuban families was scheduled to depart from Miami.
The aid will be distributed directly through the Catholic Church rather than the Cuban government. Washington said the arrangement is intended to ensure the supplies reach those in need and prevent any diversion or misuse by Cuban authorities.
United States and @CatholicRelief Services Send First Humanitarian Flight to Cuba Under @SecRubio's New $100 Million Assistance Commitment.
— Bureau of Western Hemisphere Affairs (@WHAAsstSecty) July 21, 2026
The US has previously relied on Catholic organisations to deliver humanitarian assistance to Cuba. Following the devastation caused by Hurricane Melissa, the two countries coordinated the delivery of approximately $9 million worth of aid through similar channels.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio first proposed the $100 million assistance package in May, alongside a broader effort by the Trump administration to increase pressure on Cuba's communist leadership.
In recent months, Washington has tightened sanctions on the island, intensified restrictions on oil supplies and filed criminal charges against former Cuban president Raúl Castro, a long-time influential figure in the country's leadership.
The tightening of US measures has contributed to a severe fuel shortage in Cuba, which has in turn led to several nationwide power outages in recent weeks.
President Donald Trump has also suggested that Cuba could become the next focus of his administration's foreign policy after the January military operation that removed Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro, one of Havana's closest allies, from power.

news.title: Mamdani says New York City does not have legal authority to arrest Netanyahu
news.text:
Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media.

Mamdani said he believes the international criminal court warrant for Netanyahu’s arrest over alleged war crimes is justified, but admitted that he lacks the legal authority to enforce it.

“My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the international criminal court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant.

“The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” the mayor added. “And I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large.”

Netanyahu is expected to travel to New York in September for the United Nations general assembly.

Mamdani said last week he was reviewing whether he would be able to arrest Netanyahu during that visit, after pledging to do so during his mayoral campaign last year.

“I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said in reference to the Dutch city where the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024, is located.

The US does not recognize the ICC’s authority and is not a party to it.

His remarks drew rebuke from Donald Trump, who insisted Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil.

Trump said in a social media post Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”.

Netanyahu, meanwhile, said he’s “not concerned” about Mamdani’s comments.
news.summary:
Mayor had said he was reviewing ability to execute arrest warrant issued by the ICC for Israeli prime minister

Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media.

news.author: Robert Mackey

news.title: Army Soldier, Missing in Jordan, Now Presumed Dead
news.text:
A United States soldier previously reported missing after an attack in Jordan is now believed to have been killed in action, the Department of War stated Tuesday.

He has been identified as Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York, and is believed to have died during an enemy attack on July 17 at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan.

U.S. Central Command had previously reported Rampersad as missing following the attack.

Rampersad’s death raises the total number of U.S. troops killed in the Iran war to 18 since Operation Epic Fury began on Feb. 28.

Earlier Tuesday, the Pentagon identified two other soldiers who were killed on July 17 in action at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan to be 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales. They were killed by Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) ballistic missile and drone attacks on the air base.

Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of Fayetteville, North Carolina, was killed in action at Erbil Air Base in Iraq on July 19. Swinton died during a controlled detonation of an unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian one-way attack drone.

President Donald Trump responded to news of the U.S. soldiers’ deaths with a post on Truth Social on July 20.

“Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over!” Trump wrote. “This directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military.”
news.author: Tom Gantert
